commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=20b124ce4e189f52ab650127dc360ad6313be3aa)
commands.review_summary:
The PR replaces iMessage RPC stdout `node:readline` parsing with a `StringDecoder`-backed LF-only buffer and adds client tests for Unicode separator and split UTF-8 responses.

PR surface: Source +8, Tests +339. Total +347 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source/dependency level: current main and `v2026.6.1` use readline on `imsg rpc` stdout, and a local Node probe shows U+2028/U+2029 are readline terminators. The new shutdown regression is also source-reproducible with a late stdout event after `decoder` is cleared.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Maintainer or contributor follow-up is needed because the PR has a concrete shutdown bug and the external real-environment proof gate cannot be satisfied by an automated repair worker alone.

target.title: fix(imessage): replace node:readline with LF-only buffer to prevent U+2028 splitting JSON-RPC responses
target.body:
Summary

Fixes #89830. Node readline treats U+2028 (LINE SEPARATOR) and U+2029 (PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR) as line terminators in addition to \n. When an incoming iMessage body contains raw U+2028 — which imsg emits verbatim inside JSON string values — a single valid JSON-RPC response is split into fragments that all fail JSON.parse. The pending RPC id is never resolved, and the poll loop re-fetches the same history window on every tick, producing a steady stream of parse errors and zero successful iMessage receives (head-of-line blocking).

Fix

Replace node:readline createInterface in IMessageRpcClient.start() with manual stdout buffering that splits only on \n (U+000A), backed by node:string_decoder StringDecoder for correct cross-chunk UTF-8 reassembly. This preserves the one-JSON-object-per-LF framing contract while keeping U+2028/U+2029 inside JSON string values intact and prevents silent corruption of multi-byte characters (emoji, CJK) split across stdout chunks.

Key changes in extensions/imessage/src/client.ts:

- Remove node:readline import and Interface member
- Add private decoder: StringDecoder field for streaming UTF-8 decode
- Use child.stdout.on("data", ...) with decoder.write(chunk) + indexOf("\n") splitting instead of readline line events
- Clear buffer and decoder in stop() instead of closing reader

Tests

New extensions/imessage/src/client.test.ts (16 tests):

- U+2028/U+2029 handling: verifies LF-only splitting preserves JSON strings containing Unicode line/paragraph separators
- LF framing: multi-response splitting, cross-chunk buffering, empty line skipping
- handleLine: pending request resolution, error rejection, notification dispatch, unparseable input
- Realistic scenario: simulates imsg messages.history response with U+2028 in message text and verifies the pending request resolves correctly
- UTF-8 multi-byte split across chunks (new, #89883): emoji, U+2028, CJK characters split at arbitrary byte boundaries are reassembled correctly by StringDecoder
- Naive toString comparison: demonstrates that independent chunk.toString("utf-8") produces replacement characters (�) while StringDecoder preserves the original character

All 28 iMessage tests pass (16 new + 12 existing).

Validation

# Tests
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-imessage.config.ts extensions/imessage/src/client.test.ts
→ 16 passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-imessage.config.ts extensions/imessage/src/status.test.ts
→ 12 passed

# Lint
npx oxlint extensions/imessage/src/client.ts extensions/imessage/src/client.test.ts
→ 0 warnings, 0 errors

# Type check
npx tsc --noEmit -p extensions/imessage/tsconfig.json
→ no errors

Real behavior proof

Test environment
- macOS 15.7.7, MacBook Air (M4)
- Node.js v22.22.3
- imsg v0.11.0 (basic mode, SIP enabled)
- Branch: fix/imsg-u2028-readline-89830

What was tested
1. Unit tests (extensions/imessage/src/client.test.ts)
   - 28 tests pass (16 new + 12 existing)
   - New tests cover:
     - U+2028/U+2029 inside JSON string → correctly preserved, not split
     - Chunk boundary splits across U+2028 bytes → correctly buffered via StringDecoder
     - Emoji (🎉) and CJK (中) split across chunk boundaries → correctly reassembled
     - Naive toString("utf-8") vs StringDecoder comparison → proves replacement character issue
     - Pending request resolve/reject still work

2. End-to-end Gateway verification
   - Gateway starts successfully with iMessage channel
   - Inbound/outbound message flow works normally with real imsg
   - Gateway correctly receives and replies to messages via iMessage

Why we could not test with a real U+2028 iMessage message

After extensive testing, we confirmed that macOS/iOS Messages.app filters U+2028 (LINE SEPARATOR) at the UI/input layer before the message reaches chat.db. Whether entered manually, pasted from clipboard, or sent via `imsg send` CLI, U+2028 is either removed or normalized to a regular newline by the Apple client. This is Apple platform behavior, not something imsg or Gateway can control.

However, the bug is in the JSON-RPC framing layer (client.ts reading imsg stdout), not the iMessage protocol itself. As confirmed by PR #89849, this is a Node v26-specific behavior where readline treats U+2028/U+2029 as line terminators. The unit tests and StringDecoder fix directly exercise and protect the exact code path that would be hit if imsg ever emitted a JSON-RPC frame containing these characters.

What wasn't tested
- Real iMessage message containing raw U+2028 (client-side filtered by Apple)

Conclusion

The fix removes node:readline and replaces it with a manual \n-only buffer backed by StringDecoder. This correctly handles:
1. Any Unicode character (including U+2028/U+2029) inside JSON-RPC frames — no fragmentation
2. Multi-byte UTF-8 characters split across stdout chunks — no silent replacement-character corruption

The unit tests provide sufficient coverage for both the original U+2028 bug and the cross-chunk UTF-8 decoding issue identified by @clawsweeper.
